title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Chugiak angler in Kona tops list with giant tuna By JIM RIZUTTO Special to the Daily News Published: February 26, 2006 Last Modified: February 26, 2006 at 05:52 AM On his first fishing trip in Kona, Hawaii, Jim Hagee of Chugiak boated a 28.5-pound skipjack tuna to top the Big-Fish list in the aku category. One pound larger than the biggest skipjack of 2005, it's the largest skipjack caught in Kona since a 29.5-pounder in May of 2003. Skipjacks weighing more than 20 pounds are usually referred to by the Japanese name "otaru," meaning barrel. When they get that big, the barrel-chested tuna develop a big-game ego. They go for the larger lures (this one took an Aloha Lures "Super Ninja" made for billfish and big yellowfin tuna) and fight like bruisers twice their size. "Jim's fish hit the long…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Shooting accident may be as Cheney claims -- or not Published: February 26, 2006 Last Modified: February 26, 2006 at 06:09 AM Down in Texas two weeks ago, poor old Harry Whittington learned the hard way why you don't sneak up on your companions when bird hunting. Then again, maybe not. Who can tell? The comments of the people involved in the now infamous "Deadeye Dick" shooting -- as one tabloid labeled this affair -- are so vague, the investigation so shoddy, it's hard to figure out much of anything other than that Vice President Dick Cheney swung his shotgun on a quail, then shot a 78-year-old attorney in the upper body. Did Cheney get his quail? Nobody knows, but it seems unlikely given that most of the shot from his gun appears to have "peppered" Harry, as ranch owner and hunt leader K…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Outdoors hot line Published: February 12, 2006 Last Modified: February 12, 2006 at 04:57 AM ARCHERY Black Sheep club will hold 3D shoot at Elmendorf Black Sheep Archery Club offers a 3D archery shoot today at the Black Sheep Bowmen 3D Archery Range on Six-Mile Lake at Elmendorf Air Force Base. Registration is at 9 a.m. and competition begins at 10 a.m.; $10 for nonmembers and $8 for members. Nonmembers should be at the Boniface gate no later than 8:30 a.m. to get on base. For more details, call 753-1855 or visit www.blacksheeparchery.com . CLIMBING Club meeting features Alaska Range slideshow The next meeting of the Mountineering Club of Alaska is 7:30-9 p.m. Wednesday at First United Methodist Church, Ninth Avenue and G Street. Climber Jay Rowe will present slides from his climbing experi…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Real anglers never quit Some folks would rather freeze than quit fishing for the winter By TONY LEWIS Special to the Daily News Published: February 19, 2006 Last Modified: February 20, 2006 at 04:14 AM After he had been fly-fishing two hours in single-digit temperatures, ice had clogged the bottom guides of Richard Kelso's rod, gripping the fly-line like a rope in a vise. One of the Kenai River's few die-hard winter fly-fishermen, Kelso had dealt with icing problems before. But his usual tricks to free the line were not working. He dipped the rod into the river, hoping the flowing water would free the frozen guides. But a two-week cold snap had dropped the river's temperature so close to the freezing point that its thawing powers were zapped. He picked at the stubborn ice with his gloved f…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Kasilof, Kenai kings getting a closer look STUDIES: Fish and Game wants to know more about late-run salmon returns. By PATRICE KOHL Peninsula Clarion Published: February 19, 2006 Last Modified: February 19, 2006 at 04:43 AM KENAI -- The Alaska Department of Fish and Game is gearing up for a spurt in fisheries research projects this summer, including a study to gauge the first estimates of late-run king salmon numbers in the Kasilof River. "These fish are valuable, extremely valuable," said Tim McKinley, a research biologist for the sportfish division of Fish and Game. "(And) the better and finer tuned we can have our management the more likely we are to have healthy runs." McKinley said a king salmon counting project in the Kasilof River and a Kenai River king salmon genetic sampling proje…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred Anyone who completes Iron Dog is a winner CRAIG MEDRED Outdoors Published: February 19, 2006 Last Modified: February 19, 2006 at 04:45 AM BIG LAKE -- The thing about the easy life is that it's so easy, no matter how we might all fret over how hectic it is. Americans, in particular, have come a long way in a short time. The historic norms of human survival are artifacts of the past. No longer do we spend large parts of our lives uncomfortably losing battles against the elements. No longer do we need to find wood to feed fires to stay warm, or worry about where our next meal might come from, or sit around telling stories all night to stave off the boredom. Now we can grab the hot-and-tasty fettucine ready to go at the Southside Bistro on the way home, snatch a cold beer out of t…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Foundation's intent is to foster Alaska's bears CONSERVATION: Blacks, browns on top of pyramid of healthy streams and fish. By TONY CARROLL The Juneau Empire Published: February 19, 2006 Last Modified: February 19, 2006 at 04:43 AM JUNEAU -- Alaska's bears are a powerful lot in the wild, but they're politically and ecologically vulnerable, the North American Bear Foundation says. In an effort to conserve the environment that feeds them and to insulate them from political whims, the group has come to Alaska. "The governor shouldn't be making decisions on bears," said Carl Rosier, president of the group's new Alaska chapter and a former Alaska Fish and Game commissioner. He referred to Alaska's predator-control program. Rosier, who served as commissioner from 1991 to 1995 under Gov. Walter H…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Yukon kings appear to be getting smaller SIZE: Circumstantial evidence is compelling despite lack of study. By CHRIS TALBOT Fairbanks Daily News-Miner Published: January 29, 2006 Last Modified: January 29, 2006 at 06:05 AM FAIRBANKS -- Tanana fisherman Charlie Campbell doesn't need scientific studies or empirical data to prove Yukon River chinook salmon -- the mighty king of the species -- are getting smaller. All he has to do is walk into his smokehouse. "I'm noticing I have a lot more headroom in the smokehouse than I remember," said Campbell, a subsistence fisherman who uses a fish wheel in the Rapids area of the Yukon between Tanana and Rampart. "Long salmon strips hang in your face and you have to duck to get underneath them," he said. "I'm 6-2, so it's an issue for me. With these sho…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Pike push fingerlings from lake SCOUT LAKE: Baby cohos, trout becoming meals for pest fish. The Associated Press Published: January 16, 2006 Last Modified: January 16, 2006 at 12:54 AM KENAI -- Northern pike have been discovered in a Kenai Peninsula lake, forcing fisheries officials to make new plans for fingerlings that would have been used to stock it. "We would just be feeding the pike," said biologist Patty Berkhahn about plans to send the baby fish to other sites instead of Scout Lake. Fingerlings will instead go to other central Peninsula lakes, said Berkhahn, a fisheries biologist with the Alaska Department of Fish and Game. Fish and Game biologists discovered pike in Scout Lake while sampling it for coho salmon and rainbow trout in September. Pike is considered a pest because it ea…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Athletes aren't limited by darkness By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: February 12, 2006 Last Modified: February 12, 2006 at 04:14 AM Long before the sun comes up and long after it goes down, on pitch-black subarctic nights, they emerge into the long, cold, dark across Alaska. Old or young, short or tall, male or female, lean or a few pounds overweight, these Alaskans are out there -- runners, skiers, snowshoers, adventure racers, cyclists, climbers and more. A few are training for something specific. Amy Murphy, for instance, aims to do the Annapurna Circuit, a 186-mile loop through the Himalayas that climbs to 17,700 feet. To survive, Murphy, who sometimes leads trips for the Mountaineering Club of Alaska, knows she must feed the monster of aerobic fitness. The monster deman…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: BACKCOUNTRY SAFETY Registration has begun for First Responder class Wilderness Medical Associates will conduct an 80-hour Wilderness First Responder class in Anchorage beginning Friday Feb. 3, and continuing over two three-day weekends and evenings through Feb. 12. The price is $550, which includes all books, lab materials and certifications. To register, e-mail debajango@att.net or call Michael Crotteau at 646-4811. Free 'Snow Smart' event set at Alpenglow Ski Area The North America Outdoor Institute will host a "Be Snow Smart" day 10 a.m.-6 p.m. Saturday at Alpenglow at Arctic Valley. The free event includes instruction by world champion heliskier Dean Cummings, as well as prizes and discounted tickets. For details, call 1-907-835-8418 or 1-907-841-4457. title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: ASTRONOMY Nature Center program examines night sky The popular amateur astronomy program continues at 7 p.m. Friday at the Eagle River Nature Center. The program includes an indoor program and sky viewing if the weather is clear. Bring binoculars or telescopes. Due to space constraints, groups of 10 or more should call Carole Lloyd at 694-9255 to make an appointment for a class. Youngsters can earn a "Junior Astronomer" certificate after attending six programs. (Get a punch card at the front desk.) The program is free; parking is $5 for nonmembers. For additional details, call 694-2108. CAMPING Free program teaches camping during winter Families can learn how to use cabins and yurts for winter camping at 2 p.m. today at the Eagle River Nature Center. title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: CRAIG MEDRED Old Joe set standards others may never beat Published: February 12, 2006 Last Modified: February 12, 2006 at 04:55 AM Plenty of people called the late Joe Redington Sr. "Old Joe," but no one ever really thought of him as old. He was more like timeless -- this elfin, little guy who didn't start doing the Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race until well past middle age and then kept going and going and going. Old Joe never won the race he almost single-handedly birthed, but he was often in the hunt. At a time when others of his generation were shopping for rocking chairs, he was a contender -- and a legitimate one. Retired Iditarod veteran Vern Halter from Willow got me to thinking about this the other day. We were discussing changes that have come to the Iditarod the past couple years a…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Warm weather promises hot ice-fishing jamboree weekend JEWEL LAKE: Annual event is on, no matter what the temperature. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: December 8, 2005 Last Modified: December 8, 2005 at 04:52 AM Not rain nor wind nor dark of winter day will stop the 18th Annual Ice Fishing Jamboree at Jewel Lake this weekend. As rain fell across the city Wednesday and the thermometer climbed toward a very unwinterlike 40 degrees in parts of town, event organizer Richard Freisinger said, "It's all on. "It's been so cold," he added, "that this is kind of refreshing." Fortunately, Freisinger said, a lengthy November cold snap created plenty of ice on the South Anchorage lake. Thickness runs 10 to 11 inches. When frigid weather held the city in its grasp a week ago, Freisinger …

title: Destroying trail markers will lead  to fatalities (1-30-2005)
summary: No one knows who's trying to kill the snowmachine riders of the western Susitna Valley, but it's clear someone is sowing the seeds of tragedy. All along the Susitna and Yentna rivers, someone has been mowing down the trail markers so carefully placed by the trail-breakers of the Lower Susitna Drainage Association. Often these markers guide the way around the open leads that proliferate on the rivers this year. Destroying the trail markers is tantamount to taking down the "Do Not Enter'' signs on one-way streets in Anchorage. Without such signs, someone is sure to turn the wrong way onto a one-way street and collide with oncoming traffic. Without the river markers, it's only a matter of time before someone puts their snowmobile in open water and dies. The best-case scenario is that the dang…

title: Ice houses add an inside angle to sport of wintertime fishing
summary: FAIRBANKS -- As 28-year-old Shane Shepard and his mother, Charlotte Montajes, sat inside an ice-fishing house on Chena Lake, trying to attract the interest of fish lurking below 3 feet of ice, the sound of an approaching vehicle's tires crunching on the snow and ice could be heard outside. It was room service. "The wood guy is here," said Shep-ard, peeking out one of two small windows in the shack. Grabbing a plastic five-gallon bucket, Shepard stepped out of the heated ice hut into the minus 20-degree temperatures. The wood guy, a.k.a. Larry Newberg, a recreational specialist at Chena Lake Recreation Area, pulled up next to the plywood ice house in a pickup with the Fairbanks North Star Borough logo on the door. "Want another load?" inquired Newberg, popping down the tailgate on the truck…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Record-setting BASE jumper keeps hopping, even in slow season ADRENALINE: There's still time for family between dives to Earth. By MATT CHRISTENSEN Idaho Times-News Published: February 5, 2006 Last Modified: February 5, 2006 at 03:43 AM TWIN FALLS, Idaho -- Miles Daisher, the 36-year-old man who set the unofficial world record for most human-powered BASE jumps in September, is on vacation. Well, sort of. After training for nearly a year for the event -- he jumped off the Perrine Bridge in southern Idaho 57 times in 24 hours and hiked out each time -- Miles planned to take a break. The Perrine spans the Snake River near Twin Falls and is the only U.S. bridge that allows the short-drop parachute skydiving sport known as BASE jumping (BASE stands for building, antenna, span, Earth) year-round…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Winter kayaking ambition puts friends in cold water STAYING AFLOAT: Five bundle up to find fun, seasonal beauty. By PATRICE KOHL Peninsula Clarion Published: February 5, 2006 Last Modified: February 5, 2006 at 03:43 AM KENAI -- While some watersport enthusiasts are at home sulking during this season, five women have discovered some unusual wintertime recreation. Julie Nichols of Kasilof usually retires her kayak in September or October, but mild winter temperatures sometimes leave waterways open to a temptation she cannot resist. "If you can't ski, why not?" Nichols said. "It's just another way to get out and enjoy living in Alaska. I don't think people do that enough." When Nichols recently asked her friends to go kayaking in January, they thought she was crazy. "I'm sort of afraid of the…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Cold days have us envisioning warm Hawaiian escape SCOTT MCMURREN TRAVEL Published: January 22, 2006 Last Modified: January 22, 2006 at 05:32 AM I could not help but think my plane was going in the wrong direction Friday morning. When I boarded in Anchorage, the temperature was around zero. Getting off in Fairbanks, my spine stiffened at the cool breeze. It was 30 below zero. Later in the day, strolling along the Yukon River in Beaver, we checked the temperature. It was 20 degrees cooler: minus 58. If I had waited a couple more hours in Anchorage, I could've caught Northwest's nonstop flight to Maui. Weather like this just drives Alaskans to Hawaii in droves. It is no mystery that this year both Hawaiian Vacations and Northwest Airlines are offering nonstop service between Anchorage and Ha…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Love affair with air Ski-jumping's the sport of choice as Alaska adrenaline junkies hit the slopes for a natural high By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: January 29, 2006 Last Modified: January 29, 2006 at 07:22 AM Pipe or park, snowboard or skis, free-skiing or free-riding, it's all about catching air on the slopes these days. "It's a lot of fun, because you get a little rush,'' said Anchorage snowboarder Sarah Taylor. "I'm really into powder and air,'' she added, though she confesses her enthusiasm for the latter has waned as she's aged. "I'm 28 years old,'' Taylor said. "I've already had one knee replacement. I don't need another knee replacement. "I just take calculated risks now. I'm more conservative. I'm just not going to throw myself off something if I can't see what th…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Authors' lies do readers no favors Published: February 5, 2006 Last Modified: February 5, 2006 at 04:06 AM The memoir of the drunkard or druggie who finds salvation in some strange or mysterious way has become such a cliche of modern American literature that one can't help but wonder if there is much difference between disgraced writer James Frey and many other scribblers out there -- except that Frey hit pay dirt with a guest appearance on Oprah and a best seller. Frey is the author of "A Million Little Pieces,'' a lurid, jerkily written tale of drugs, sex, crime, personal decay and recovery that publishers wouldn't buy as a novel. The book was subsequently transformed into a memoir that made Frey one of the more successful storytellers of the day until the Web site www.thes…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Aklestad, Tyson plan full-throttle attack for Iron Dog title By BRIAN SINGLER Anchorage Daily News Published: February 5, 2006 Last Modified: February 5, 2006 at 06:49 AM A year ago, a battered Tyson Johnson was unable to stand. Now, the young but veteran snowmachine racer is once again eyeing the winner's podium waiting near the Fairbanks finish of the Tesoro Iron Dog snowmobile race. At the ripe old age of 26, Johnson is starting over with a new partner, a new machine and another chance to win the world's longest, wildest and toughest snowmobile race. The Iron Dog, which starts Feb. 12, roars across 1,851 miles of largely untracked wilderness from Big Lake north over the Alaska Range to Nome and then back along the Yukon and other Interior rivers to Fairbanks. Having nearly lost everythi…

title: Craig Medred: Think this is cold? Quit whining; it's been worse
summary: The whining about the subzero cold settled over Southcentral last week was almost enough to make one wonder if Anchorage residents still qualify as Alaskans. "My skate skis won't slide at 10 degrees below zero. It's like skating in sand. What am I supposed to do? Wah, wah, wah.'' What's going on here? Has too much Seattle blood infiltrated the gene pool? Would you prefer the rain of last winter? Life is hard, people. It's always been hard, particularly here in the Northland. Does anyone remember what it was like when you had to break your own trail on those heavy, old classical skis to get to the cabin where you had to chop wood so you could struggle to start a fire that would eventually warm the place to something above freezing and you could start the chore of cooking something to eat? N…

summary: Outdoors hot line SITE OF THE WEEK Published: January 22, 2006 Last Modified: January 22, 2006 at 05:32 AM Backcountry SAFETY First Responder class registration has begun Wilderness Medical Associates will conduct an 80-hour Wilderness First Responder class in Anchorage Feb. 3-12, over two three-day weekends and evenings. The price is $550, which includes all books, lab materials and certifications. To register, e-mail debajango@att.net or call Michael Crotteau at 646-4811. BOATING Kayaking skills class offered at Service High Alaska Kayak School offers a skills course today at the Service High pool. Learn and practice introductory and intermediate kayaking skills. Beginners meet 11 a.m.-2:30 p.m. The intermediate group meets 3-7 p.m. To register, go to REI, call the kayak school at 1-907-…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Lack of snow keeps machines off trails Daily News staff Published: January 10, 2006 Last Modified: January 10, 2006 at 01:48 AM Land managers in the Anchorage and Mat-Su area and on the western Kenai Peninsula continue to keep recreation areas closed to motorized use because of an inadequate snowpack. All areas of Chugach State Park remain closed to snowmachining "due to the lack of snow and continued exposed vegetation," the park's managers said Friday in a written statement. Some trails out of snowmachine-access areas in the park were down to ice and rock, they said. Meanwhile, the Seward and Glacier ranger districts of Chugach National Forest -- essentially Twentymile Valley to Turnagain Pass and all places in between, as well as all other valleys on the west side of the peninsula -- we…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Outdoors hot line Published: January 8, 2006 Last Modified: January 8, 2006 at 02:40 AM ASTRONOMY Weekly astronomy program explores myths of the stars "Myths and Legends of Stars" is the topic of this week's astronomy series at 7 p.m. Thursday in the Campbell Creek Science Center, 6881 Abbott Loop Road. There are 88 recognized constellations, many of which have a legend or myth associated with them. But just who was Orion? How do you get a bear from the Big Dipper? Learn from Kathy East, who has been teaching astronomy for several years and is a member of the Chugach Star Gazer Society. Participants will work with star maps to identify constellations and then, weather permitting, go outside for some hands-on observation. For details, call 267-1247.
